serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Gouda cheese is approximately 30 g (one thin slice), commonly used on crackers, in sandwiches, or as part of a cheese board.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese, gouda — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173420)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for Gouda cheese used as primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are consistent with USDA data and are retained. Saturated fat (18.5 g/100 g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient detail sourced from USDA SR Legacy where available.

gi_source.title: N/A — Gouda cheese contains negligible carbohydrates (1.4 g/100 g); glycemic index is not applicable for this food type
gi_source.source_match_notes: Gouda cheese is a semi-hard dairy product with only 1.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and 0 g sugar. Hard and semi-hard cheeses are not listed in the University of Sydney GI Database because glycemic index testing is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. The numeric value 0 is used as a sentinel to satisfy template numeric requirements; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to clarify that GI testing does not apply. GL per 30 g serving = 0.0 (carbohydrate contribution is below threshold for meaningful GL calculation).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 800 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper limit for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Gouda cheese to very small portions (15–20 g) or choose low-sodium cheese alternatives such as fresh ricotta or mozzarella, as the sodium content of 800 mg/100 g substantially raises daily sodium load and can elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (1.4 g/100 g) and zero sugar; high protein (25.8 g/100 g) supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gouda cheese has minimal glycaemic impact due to negligible carbohydrate and zero sugar content; however, portions should be limited to 30 g due to high saturated fat (18.5 g/100 g), which may worsen insulin resistance if consumed in excess.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (18.5 g/100 g) and high sodium (800 mg/100 g) are both adverse cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict Gouda cheese to occasional small portions (30 g or less) given its high saturated fat content (18.5 g/100 g), which raises LDL cholesterol, and its high sodium content (800 mg/100 g), which contributes to hypertension and increased cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 18.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; sodium 800 mg/100 g worsens joint oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Gouda cheese is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to high saturated fat (18.5 g/100 g), which promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and high sodium (800 mg/100 g), which worsens joint-related oedema; low-fat, low-sodium dairy alternatives are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gouda cheese is a semi-hard Dutch cheese originating from the city of Gouda in the Netherlands. It is made by curdling pasteurised cow's milk with rennet, cutting the curd, washing it with warm water to reduce acidity, pressing the curds into round moulds, brining in salt solution, and then aging for periods ranging from a few weeks (young Gouda) to several years (aged or extra-aged Gouda). The texture is smooth and supple in young varieties, becoming firmer, drier, and more crumbly with extended aging. The flavour profile is mild, sweet, and slightly nutty when young, intensifying to a rich, caramel-like, and complex taste in aged forms. Nutritionally, Gouda is energy-dense at approximately 380 kcal per 100 g, with high protein (25.8 g/100 g) and high fat (29 g/100 g), of which saturated fat constitutes approximately 18.5 g/100 g. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 1.4 g/100 g with zero sugar, making it essentially non-glycaemic. Sodium is high at 800 mg/100 g, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Calcium content is notably high at 680 mg/100 g, supporting bone health. Gouda is widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia as a snack, in sandwiches, on cheese boards, and melted in cooked dishes. It is not suitable for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis in regular or large portions, but can be consumed in small amounts (30 g) by individuals managing type 2 diabetes given its negligible carbohydrate content.

notes: Sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for Gouda cheese — lactose is largely eliminated during the cheesemaking and aging process. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as Gouda is an animal-derived dairy product containing no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible per USDA SR Legacy). Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — cheese contains no ascorbic acid). Monounsaturated_fat_g (7.7 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g/100 g), and all micronutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173420. Gouda cheese is a semi-hard dairy product; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.4 g/100 g). gi_category is set to 'not_applicable'. The numeric value 0 is used in the glycemic_index field as a template-required numeric sentinel; the gi_source and gi_category fields clarify that GI testing does not apply to this food. The input dietary_tag 'plant_based' has been removed as Gouda is an animal-derived dairy product and is not plant-based. Country of origin is Netherlands (Gouda, South Holland). Halal status is not confirmed as standard Gouda uses animal rennet; consumers requiring halal-certified cheese should verify the specific product.
